title: Factorial Calculator
description:
Write a recursive method called `factorial` that computes n! (n factorial).

Remember: n! = n × (n-1) × (n-2) × ... × 2 × 1, and 0! = 1 by definition.

For example:
- factorial(5) → 120 (because 5 × 4 × 3 × 2 × 1 = 120)
- factorial(0) → 1
- factorial(7) → 5040

Your method should call itself — no loops allowed!
hints: What's the simplest factorial you know the answer to without calculating? That's your base case! (Hint: 0! = 1)
The recursive pattern is: factorial(n) = n * factorial(n - 1). You're trusting that factorial(n-1) will give you the right answer.
Base case: if (n == 0) return 1; Recursive case: return n * factorial(n - 1);

title: Fibonacci with Tracing
description:
Write a recursive method called `fib` that computes the nth Fibonacci number AND prints a trace message each time it is called.

The Fibonacci sequence: 0, 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 21, ...
- fib(0) = 0
- fib(1) = 1
- fib(n) = fib(n-1) + fib(n-2) for n ≥ 2

Before doing any computation, print: fib(n) called (where n is the actual argument).

This tracing will help you SEE how many times the method gets called — it's more than you think!
expectedOutput:
fib(4) called
fib(3) called
fib(2) called
fib(1) called
fib(0) called
fib(1) called
fib(2) called
fib(1) called
fib(0) called
Result: 3
hints: Start every call with: System.out.println("fib(" + n + ") called"); — this goes BEFORE the base case check.
You need TWO base cases: if n is 0 return 0, if n is 1 return 1. Or combine them: if (n <= 1) return n;
Full method: print the trace, then if (n <= 1) return n; then return fib(n - 1) + fib(n - 2); Notice how fib(4) generates 9 calls total — that's the exponential blowup!

title: Reverse a String Recursively
description:
Write a recursive method called `reverse` that takes a String and returns it backwards.

For example:
- reverse("hello") → "olleh"
- reverse("Java") → "avaJ"
- reverse("a") → "a"

You cannot use loops, StringBuilder, or any built-in reverse methods. Only recursion, charAt(), and substring()!

Think about it this way: reversing "hello" is the same as reversing "ello" and then sticking 'h' at the end.
hints: Base case: if the string has 0 or 1 characters, it's already reversed — just return it as-is.
s.charAt(0) gives you the first character. s.substring(1) gives you everything AFTER the first character. To reverse, you want to reverse the rest and then add the first character at the end.
return reverse(s.substring(1)) + s.charAt(0); This recursively reverses everything after the first character, then appends the first character to the end.

title: Sum of Digits
description:
Write a recursive method called `sumDigits` that takes a non-negative integer and returns the sum of all its digits.

For example:
- sumDigits(1234) → 10 (because 1 + 2 + 3 + 4 = 10)
- sumDigits(9) → 9
- sumDigits(505) → 10 (because 5 + 0 + 5 = 10)

Hint: You can extract the last digit of a number with n % 10, and remove the last digit with n / 10.

No converting to String allowed — use math only!
hints: If the number is a single digit (less than 10), the sum is just the number itself — that's your base case!
n % 10 gives the last digit. n / 10 gives the number without its last digit. For example: 1234 % 10 = 4, and 1234 / 10 = 123.
return n % 10 + sumDigits(n / 10); This grabs the last digit and adds it to the sum of all the other digits. Base case: if (n < 10) return n;

title: Power Function
description:
Write a recursive method called `power` that computes x raised to the nth power (x^n).

Your method must handle THREE cases:
1. Any number to the power of 0 is 1 (base case)
2. Positive exponents: x^n = x × x^(n-1)
3. Negative exponents: x^(-n) = 1 / x^n

For example:
- power(2, 10) → 1024.0
- power(3, 0) → 1.0
- power(2, -3) → 0.125 (because 1/8 = 0.125)
- power(5, 3) → 125.0

Use `double` for the base and return type so negative exponents work correctly.
hints: Start with the base case: if exp == 0, return 1. Then think about what to do when exp is negative.
For negative exponents, flip it: power(base, -3) is the same as 1.0 / power(base, 3). You only need to handle this once, then let the positive case handle the rest.
Three cases: if (exp == 0) return 1; if (exp < 0) return 1.0 / power(base, -exp); return base * power(base, exp - 1);
